content.text:
{I will give you the treasures of darkness and the hoards in secret places.}{Isaiah 45:3}
 {The diagonal form of a matrix is like the skeleton of the matrix, stripped of all that is inessential.}{— Sheldon Axler}

A matrix is **diagonalizable** if it can be expressed in terms 
of a diagonal matrix.

content.title: Diagonalizable
content.content:
An $n \times n$ matrix $A$ is **diagonalizable** 
if there exists an invertible matrix $P$ and a diagonal matrix $D$ such that
\[
A = PDP\inv
\]
Equivalently, $P\inv AP = D$.

content.title: Diagonalization Theorem
content.content:
An $n \times n$ matrix $A$ is diagonalizable if and only if $A$ has 
$n$ linearly independent eigenvectors.

If so, the columns of $P$ are the eigenvectors, and the diagonal 
entries of $D$ are the corresponding eigenvalues.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Diagonalizing a matrix
content.solution:
From Section [sec:characteristic]: eigenvalues $2, 3$ with 
eigenvectors $\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, 
$\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
\[
P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
D = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
\]
Verify: $A = PDP\inv$.

content.text:
For a diagonal matrix, $D^k$ is trivial: just raise each diagonal 
entry to the $k$-th power.

marginNotes.text:
He reveals deep and hidden things; he 
knows what is in the darkness, and the light dwells with him.
